I. 
JUDGMENT: 
1. This is the judgment of the Community Court of Justice ECOWAS 
(hereinafter referred to as 'the Court') delivered virtually and in open Court 
under Article 8( 1) of the Practice Direction on Electronic Case Management 
and Virtual Court Session, 2020. 
II. 
DESCRIPTION OF THE PARTIES: 
2. The First Applicant is Mr. Babacar Ndiaye is a community citizen resident in 
the Federal Republic of Nigeria Inter-Governmental Action Group (GIABA) 
situated in Dakar, Senegal. 
3. The Second Applicant is Mr. Modibo Z. Usman, is a community citizen 
resident in the Federal Republic of Nigeria and an employee of the Inter-
Governmental Action Group (GIABA) situated in Dakar, Senegal. 
4. The Third Applicant is Mr. Alhaji Barry a community citizen resident in 
BanjuJ, the Gambia and an employee of the Inter-Governmental Action Group 
(GIABA) situated in Dakar, Senegal. 
5. The Respondent is the ECO WAS Commission, an Institution of the Economic 
Community of West African States. 
6. The Second Respondent is President of the ECOWAS Commission. 
III. 
INTRODUCTION 
7. This Application is instituted by the Applicants, who were staff members of 
the Respondent institution, challenging what they describe as their unlawful 
dismissal from the service of the Respondent. The Applicants contend that 
their respective dismissals were carried out in flagrant violation of the 
ECOW AS Staff Regulations and in breach of their fundamental human rights 
41 Page 
~ M 


guaranteed under the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights and 
other applicable international human rights instruments. 
8. The Applicants allege that the Respondents, acting through its responsible 
authority, failed to adhere to the due process requirements prescribed under 
the ECOWAS Staff Regulations governing disciplinary procedures. They 
assert that no proper investigation or disciplinary hearing was conducted prior 
to the decision terminating their appointments, thereby violating their right to 
fair hearing and the principles of natural justice. 
9. The subject matter of the present application therefore centres on the illegality 
of the Applicants' dismissal, the suspension of their salaries, allowances, and 
benefits, and the resulting violation of their employment rights and human 
dignity. The Applicants invoke the provisions of Articles 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 of 
the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights, and Articles 67, 69, and 
73 of the ECO WAS Staff Regulations, as the legal bases for their claim. 
IO.In essence, the Applicants invite this Honourable Court to find that the 
Respondent's actions, by failing to comply with its own internal disciplinary 
procedures and by terminating their employment without due process, 
constitute a breach of their contractual and human rights obligations under the 
ECOW AS legal framework. 
IV. 
PROCEDURE BEFORE THE COURT 
11. The Applicants filed their Initiating Applications on diverse dates from ~ 
February 2022, 6th April 2023 and 3rd September 2023 in the Registry of the 
Court. 
12.On various dates in 2023, the Applicants severally filed a Motion for 
Expedited Procedure and a Motion for Default Judgment in the Registry of 
the Court. 
SI Page 
4- i4 


13.This was followed by a response from the Respondent on 19th June 2023 and 
5th July 2023, respectively, in the form of a Motion for Extension of Time and 
a Joint Statement of Defense. 
14. The Court held three virtual hearings on 3rd July 2024, 9th July 2024 and 24th 
September 2024, in which the Applicants were individually heard on the 
merits of their claim and the Motions for Default Judgment withdrawn. 
However, the Court ordered a consolidation of the Applicants' claims on 24th 
September 2024 by way of an order. 
V. 
APPLICANT'S CASE 
a. Summary of facts 
THE FIRST APPLICANT'S CASE 
15.The First Applicant, Mr. Babacar Ndiaye, served as Director of 
Administration with the Inter-Governmental Action Group against Money 
Laundering and Terrorism Financing (GIABA), an institution of the 
Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), headquartered in 
Dakar, Senegal. His letter of appointment, dated September 2009, indicates 
that his appointment took effect from 1st July 2011, a position he held until 
9th March 2016, when he was redeployed to the ECOWAS Commission in 
Abuja. 
16. The Applicant avers that on 11th July 2019, he was unlawfully suspended by 
the Respondent on grounds of alleged irregularities contained in a Forensic 
Audit Report concerning the procurement of IT equipment during his tenure 
at GIABA. He contends that the said suspension was predicated on a non-
existent or unsubstantiated audit report. 
61 Page 


17. The Applicant further asserts that he was not issued any formal query nor 
given an opportunity to respond to the allegations prior to his suspension. He 
maintains that for over a year following the suspension, no investigative panel 
was constituted to examine the alleged irregularities. 
18.He contends that he was subsequently summarily dismissed from the 
Respondent's service without strict compliance with the disciplinary 
procedures prescribed by the ECO WAS Staff Regulations, particularly those 
relating to disciplinary proceedings, dismissal, and appeals. 
19 .Foil owing his dismissal, the Applicant states that his salaries and emoluments 
attached to the office of the Director of Administration were withheld by the 
Second Respondent from January 2021 to date, contrary to the provisions of 
the Staff Regulations. 
20.0n 7tta October 2020, the Applicant received a letter from the First Respondent 
requesting clarification regarding alleged irregularities discovered in 
connection with calls for tender at GIABA, pursuant to a forensic audit. The 
Applicant responded on 8th October 2020, denying wrongdoing and 
requesting access to the said audit report. 
21. The Applicant maintains that the query failed to specify any concrete act or 
omission attributable to him. He further avers that the Forensic Audit Report 
conducted by Ernst & Young was never made available to him for review or 
comment, contrary to established auditing best practices and the principles of 
due process. 
22. The Applicant states that he was invited to appear before the Joint Disciplinary 
Board (IDB) originally scheduled to meet in Abuja, but which eventually sat 
in Dakar, Senegal, from 9th to 12th November 2020, to deliberate on the said 
allegations. 
~£~ 
71 Page 


23.The Applicant challenges the constitution and composition of the IDB, 
alleging that its formation, chairmanship, and membership were irregular and 
contrary to the mandatory provisions of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations. 
24.During the IDB proceedings, the Applicant was handed a charge sheet 
outlining the alleged offences without adequate time or facilities to prepare 
his defence. He further alleges that the forensic report was withheld, despite 
repeated requests by his counsel, and that no witnesses were called, including 
representatives of the audit firm. Consequently, he was denied the opportunity 
to cross-examine or rebut the evidence against him. 
25.The Applicant avers that although eight staff members were initially indicted 
in connection with the same allegations, three were exonerated without 
explanation, which he construes as evidence of discriminatory treatment and 
a preconceived intention to remove him from service. 
26.On 19th February 2021, the Applicant lodged an appeal to the Council against 
his dismissal. However, he claims that his salaries and other benefits were 
suspended even before the determination of his appeal, which he considers a 
flagrant violation of the ECO WAS Staff Regulations. He further contends that 
the Council failed to properly consider his submissions. 
27. The Applicant notes that the Auditors' Report concluded that he failed in his 
supervisory role as Director of Administration and Finance in relation to 
procurement and payment procedures. He disputes this finding, arguing that 
the ECOW AS Financial Regulations contain no provision defining such a 
role, and that the allegations surfaced three years after his transfer from 
GIABA to the ECOW AS Commission. 
28.ln summary, the Applicant's claim rests on the alleged non-compliance by the 
Respondent with its own Staff Regulations, amounting to violations of his 
right to fair hearing, right to presumption of innoceµce, right to equal 
St Page 
~ !..:4-, 


treatment, and right to work and equal remuneration, as guaranteed under the 
African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights and other applicable legal 
instruments. 
THE SECOND APPLICANT'S CASE 
29.The Second Applicant, Mr. Alhaji Barry, was appointed as Accountant of the 
Inter-Governmental Action Group against Money Laundering and Terrorism 
Financing (GIABA), an institution of ECOWAS headquartered in Dakar, 
Senegal, by a letter of appointment dated 21 st June 2006 ( attached and marked 
Exhibit 1). 
30.On 28th September 2020, the Applicant received a query from the Respondent 
based on alleged irregularities arising from a Forensic Audit Report conducted 
by Ernst& Young (U.K.), relating to the procurement of computer equipment 
during his tenure as Accountant at GIABA ( attached as Exhibit 2). 
31.Upon receipt of the said query, the Applicant provided a detailed written 
response addressing the allegations against him by letter dated 13th October 
2022, annexed as Exhibit 3. 
32.The Applicant avers that no panel or investigative committee was constituted 
by the Respondent for over a year after the alleged incident, contrary to the 
provisions of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations which require prompt initiation 
of disciplinary proceedings. 
33.On or about 19th October 2020, the Applicant received a memorandum from 
the Second Defendant inviting him to appear before the ECO WAS 
Disciplinary Council to respond to the allegations contained in the said audit 
report. The meeting, initially scheduled to be held in Abuja, was subsequently 
relocated to Dakar, Senegal, and convened from 9th to 12th November 2020. 
9j Page 
, 


Description of the Irregularities and Disciplinary Proceedings 
34. The Applicant notes that the invitation letter was signed by the Commissioner 
for Human Resources and copied to the Chairman of the Joint Disciplinary 
Advisory Board (JDAB), a procedure which he submits is contrary to the 
ECOW AS Staff Regulations. 
35. The Applicant further contends that, prior to the constitution of the JDAB, the 
Chairman of the Board had already been appointed by the Second 
Respondent, thereby compromising the independence and neutrality of the 
Board in violation of the governing Staff Regulations. 
36. The Applicant maintains that the Joint Disciplinary Boar~ under the 
ECOWAS legal framework, is expected to operate as an independent and 
impartial body, free from any undue interference by the Second Respondent 
concerning its composition, leadership, or recommendations on disciplinary 
matters. 
3 7. The Applicant states that upon appearing before the Board on 11th November 
2020, he was served with a charge sheet outlining the alleged offences only at 
the meeting, thereby depriving him of adequate time and facilities to prepare 
his defence. 
38.He further asserts that no witnesses were called during the proceedings to 
substantiate the allegations or demonstrate his involvement in the purported 
fraudulent activities at GIABA. The Applicant was consequently denied the 
opportunity to cross-examine witnesses or challenge the evidence against him. 
39. The Applicant avers that the audit firm, Ernst & Young (U .K. ), was not invited 
to the disciplinary session, thus depriving him of an opportunity to contest its 
findings or interrogate its conclusions. 
40.Furthermore, the Ernst & Young report which allegedly formed the basis of 
the accusations was never made available to him, nor was he confronted with 
10 IP age 
~~ 


its contents, contrary to the principles of due process and the right to a fair 
hearing. 
On the Dismissal and Appeal 
41. The Applicant states that a total of seven GIABA staff members, including 
himself, were dismissed on suspicion of conspiracy to commit financial 
irregularities, namely:

42. The Applicant avers that although all the above-named individuals were 
jointly accused, Mr. Cherif Youssoupha Ndiaye and Mrs. Gina Wood were 
exonerated by the Second Respondent without any stated reasons, an act he 
considers discriminatory and inconsistent with the principles of equal 
treatment guaranteed by the ECOWAS Staff Regulations and the African 
Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights. 
111 Page 
and 


43.The Applicant was dismissed from service by letter dated 19th January 2021, 
issued in accordance with the recommendations of the Joint Disciplinary 
Board ( attached as Exhibit 5). 
44.Dissatisfied with the decision, the Applicant appealed by letter dated 26th 
January 2021, but the Second Respondent reaffirmed the dismissal in its reply, 
maintaining the disciplinary sanction (attached as Exhibit 6). 
45.The Applicant further invoked his right of final appeal under Articles 27 and 
73 of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations, by submitting a formal request for 
appeal through the Staff Representative to the Administration and Finance 
Committee for onward transmission to the Council of Ministers on 8th March 
2021. A copy of the acknowledged correspondence is annexed as Exhibit 7. 
46.By letter dated 8th February 2022, the Council of Ministers communicated its 
decision through the Director-General ofGIABA, confirming the Applicant's 
dismissal without pension rights, and directing the institution of legal 
proceedings against him for the recovery of alleged misappropriated funds. 
4 7. The Applicant asserts that even prior to the final decision on his appeal, his 
salaries, allowances, and other emoluments were suspended by the Second 
Respondent from January 2021, contrary to Article 73 of the ECOWAS Staff 
Regulations, which guarantees the right to remuneration pending the 
conclusion of an appeal. 
48. The Applicant further states that because of the unlawful cessation of his 
salary and benefits, he has suffered severe financial hardship, emotional 
distress, and psychological trauma, for which he seeks redress from this 
Honourable Court. 
THIRD APPLICANT'S CASE 
12 I Page 


49.The Third Applicant, Mr. Modibo Uzman, was a staff member of the Inter-
Governmental Action Group against Money Laundering and Terrorism 
Financing (GIABA), an institution of ECOWAS headquartered in Dakar, 
Senegal. He was appointed as a Programme Expert (ICT) by a letter of 
appointment dated 31 st August 2006, annexed hereto as Annexure A. 
SO.The Applicant avers that he was suspended on l51 July 2019 based on 
unproven, unsubstantiated, and fabricated allegations of involvement in 
alleged irregularities in the procurement process at GIABA between 2011 and 
2013. He maintains that the allegations were false and maliciously contrived 
to justify his removal from office. 
51. The Applicant asserts that the Respondent failed to comply with Article 69 of 
the ECOWAS Staff Regulations, which prescribes the procedural safeguards 
that must be followed in all disciplinary proceedings. As a result of this non-
compliance, he was unlawfully suspended and subsequently dismissed by the 
Second Respondent, with his salary stopped in January 2021. 
52. The Applicant alleges that he was not afforded the right to a fair hearing prior 
to his suspension and dismissal. Specifically, he was not issued any formal 
query or afforded an opportunity to respond to the allegations before the 
decision to suspend him was taken. Furthermore, for over a year after his 
suspension, no investigative panel was constituted to examine the allegations 
against him. 
53.It was only on 28th September 2020 that the Applicant received a query from 
the Respondent ( attached as Annexure B), alleging irregularities and 
procurement fraud. The Applicant promptly responded, denying any 
involvement and pointing out that the requisition cited did not attribute to him 
any act or omission resulting in personal gain or loss, pecuniary or otherwise, 
' 
to GIABA. 
t rJ&- ~ 
131 Page 


54.The Applicant contends that the Forensic Audit Report conducted by Ernst & 
Young (U.K.), which allegedly fonned the basis of the accusations, was never 
made available to him for review or comment, contrary to auditing best 
practices and the principles of due process. He insists that he was wrongly 
accused of procurement fraud, as he was not a staff of the procurement 
division and did not perform procurement functions. 
55.The Applicant maintains that he was never shown any document evidencing 
proof of fraud or indictment and that, despite repeated written requests, the 
Ernst & Young Audit Report was not disclosed to him. His written response 
dated 15th October 2020 is annexed hereto as Annexure C. 
56. The Applicant further alleges that, prior to the conclusion of any investigation, 
the Director-General of GIABA, by correspondence dated 15th July 2019 
(annexed as Annexure D), circulated letters to Financial Intelligence Units of 
ECOW AS Member States, the EFCC, and the FA TF, portraying him as a 
criminal. He contends that this act was intended to malign his reputation and 
prejudice the outcome of any subsequent disciplinary process. 
57. The Applicant avers that no witnesses were called during the disciplinary 
process to testify for or against him, and that Ernst & Young was not invited 
to any hearing to allow him to challenge their findings. He emphasizes that 
the audit report itself was never produced, thus depriving him of the 
opportunity to contest its contents or defend himself adequately. 
58.The Applicant states that eight staff members were accused of conspiring to 
defraud GIABA; however, three were exonerated without explanation, while 
the others, including himself, were dismissed. He interprets this as evidence 
of a clandestine scheme to dismiss him from service without proof of 
wrongdoing. He alleges further that the Director-General of GIABA acted as 
141 Page 
~ ~~-


accuser, investigator, and adjudicator, thereby compromising the fairness of 
the process and violating the principles of natural justice. 
59.The Applicant also refers to a letter dated 28th October 2019 from the 
Respondent to the contractor, LASTING SOLUTIONS, seeking clarification 
regarding the alleged irregularities. The contractor's response, annexed as 
Annexure F, completely exonerated the Applicant, confirming that he 
committed no infraction whatsoever in connection with the supply of IT 
equipment (see Annexure E and F). 
60.The Applicant concludes that the Respondent's actions, as narrated above, 
constitute gross violations of the ECO WAS Staff Regulations, particularly 
Articles 67 and 69, and further amount to breaches of his right to fair hearing 
guaranteed under the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights and 
other international human rights instruments to which the Respondent is a 
party. 
b. Pleas in law 
61. The Applicants rely on the following legal instruments, jurisprudence, and 
provisions of law in support of their claims before this Honourable Court. 
• 
Legal Basis for Jurisdiction 
62.The Applicants invoke the jurisdiction of this Honourable Court pursuant to: 
i. Article 33 of the Rules of Procedure of the Community Court of Justice; 
ii. Articles 9(f) and 10 of the Supplementary Protocol AISP.1101/05 amending 
Protocol AIP 117 /91 relating to the Community Court of Justice, which confer 
competence on the Court to hear cases of alleged violations of human rights 
occurring in any Member State of the Community. 
15 I Pa g e 


• 
Substantive Human Rights Instruments Relied Upon 
63.The Applicants allege violations of their fundamental hwnan rights 
guaranteed under the following international and regional hwnan rights 
instruments to which the Respondent is a party: 
1. 
Articles 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 7 of the African Charter on Human and 
Peoples' Rights (ACHPR), particularly Article 7 relating to the 
right to a fair hearing; 
ii. 
Articles 2, 3, 8, 12 and 25 of the Universal Declaration of Human 
Rights (UDHR, 1948); 
111. 
Articles 2, 3, 6 and 26 of the International Covenant on Civil and 
Political Rights (ICCPR); and 
1v. 
Articles 2, 3, 5, 10, 11 and 12 of the International Covenant on 
Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR). 
• Institutional and Administrative Law Provisions 
64.The Applicants further rely on provisions of the Revised ECOWAS Staff 
Regulations, particularly: 
1. 
Articles 63, 67, 69 and 73, which govern staff discipline, dismissal, 
and appeal procedures within ECOW AS institutions, and 
11. 
The Respondent's obligation to observe due process and procedural 
fairness in all disciplinary actions. 
• Grounds of Alleged Violations 
65. The Applicants allege: 
1. 
Violation of Articles 67, 69 and 73 of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations 
through failure to follow the prescribed disciplinary and appeal 
procedures. 
16 IP age 


11. 
Violation of Article 7 of the African Charter on Human and Peoples' 
Rights guaranteeing the right to a fair hearing and presumption of 
innocence. 
m. 
Violation of Articles 1 to 5 of the African Charter, concerning the right 
to equality before the law, human dignity, and protection from 
discrimination; and 
1v. 
Violation of the Applicants' right to work and to equal remuneration 
for equal work, as provided under Articles 10 and 11 of the ICESCR. 
• Supporting Jurisprudence 
66.ln further support of their pleas, the Applicants rely on the jurisprudence of 
this Honourable Court and relevant international case law upholding the 
principles of due process, equality before the law, fair hearing, and 
administrative justice in employment-related disputes within international 
organizations. 
c. Reliefs sought 
67 .Recognising that the three applications have been consolidated, the 
Applicants respectfully seek from this Honourable Court the following reliefs: 
17 IP age 
a) A Declaration that the dismissal of the Applicants by the 1st and 2nd 
Respondents is arbitrary, unlawful, null and void, having been carried 
out in violation of the ECOW AS Staff Regulations, the African Charter 
on Human and Peoples' Rights, and other applicable international 
human rights instruments to which the Respondents are bound. 
b) A Declaration that the suspension and/or stoppage of the Applicants' 
salaries, allowances and other entitlements prior to the exhaustion of 
the internal appeal process is arbitrary, unlawful, null and void, and 
~~~ 


181 Page 
contrary to the provisions of Article 68(b) of the ECOWAS Staff 
Regulations. 
c) An Order annulling and setting aside the dismissal of the Applicants 
forthwith for being arbitrary, null and void, and in violation of the 
ECOWAS Staff Regulations, the African Charter on Human and 
Peoples' Rights, and other relevant international instruments. 
d) An Order directing the Respondents to reinstate the Applicants to their 
respective positions within the ECOW AS system, with full restoration 
of all rights, privileges, and benefits accruing thereto. 
e) An Order of Mandatory Injunction restraining the Respondents, their 
agents or assigns, from advertising, reassigning, or otherwise filling 
the posts formerly occupied by the Applicants, in contravention of 
Article 73(b) of the ECO WAS Staff Regulations, pending full 
compliance with the due process of law. 
f) An Order directing the Respondents to pay forthwith to the Applicants 
all outstanding salaries, allowances, and other entitlements from 
January 2021 to the date of full compliance with judgment, including 
bonuses, grants, and other statutory benefits wrongfully withheld. 
g) In the alternative to Relief ( 6), an Order mandating the Respondents to 
pay all such outstanding sums together with adequate compensation 
for the material and moral damages suffered by the Applicants as a 
result of the unlawful actions of the Respondents, assessed in the sum 
of Five Million United States Dollars (US$5,000,000). 
h) An Order restraining the Respondents from further violating the rights 
of the Applicants in any manner whatsoever without due process of 
law. 


i) An Order directing the Respondents to pay to the Applicants the sum 
of One Hundred Thousand United States Dollars (US$100,000) jointly 
as costs of these proceedings. 
j) And for such further or other orders as this Honourable Court may 
deem fit to make in the circumstances of this case. 
VI. 
RESPONDENT'S CASE 
a. Summary of facts 
68. The Respondents filed a joint Statement of Defence denying all the allegations 
and averments contained in the Applicants' initiating application. They 
contend that the dismissals of the three Applicants were lawful,justified, and 
in full compliance with the ECOWAS Staff Regulations. 
• 
On the Allegation of Unlawful Suspension and Dismissal 
69. The Respondents deny that the Applicants were unlawfully suspended or 
dismissed. They assert that each of the Applicants was issued with a query 
dated 28th September 2020 arising from an independent Forensic Audit 
Report conducted by Ernst & Young (U .K. ), which uncovered serious 
financial and procedural irregularities at the Inter-Governmental Action 
Group against Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing (GIABA) during 
their respective tenures. 
70. The Respondents maintain that the said forensic audit revealed credible and 
irrefutable evidence of misconduct involving fraud, embezzlement, and abuse 
of trust detrimental to the interests of the Community. Accordingly, the 
Respondents acted within the powers conferred by the ECOW AS Staff 
Regulations to suspend and subsequently dismiss the Applicants from service. 
191 Pa g e 
t ~,.., 


71.They rely on Article 69(e) of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations, which 
authorizes the Head of Institution to exercise the power of summary dismissal 
where the evidence of misconduct is deemed irrefutable. They further argue 
that the ECOWAS Staff Regulations do not mandatorily require the 
establishment of an investigative panel where the facts have already been 
verified through an independent audit process. 
• 
On the Disciplinary Proceedings 
72. The Respondents state that, in full compliance with the Regulations, each 
Applicant was duly invited to appear before the Joint Disciplinary Board 
(IDB) via memoranda issued between October and November 2020, to defend 
themselves against the allegations arising from the forensic audit report. 
73. The Respondents maintain that the Joint Disciplinary Board was properly 
constituted in accordance with Article 67 of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations, 
with a duly appointed Chairman, Secretary, and members, and that the Board 
functioned independently and impartially in the exercise of its mandate. 
74. They further contend that the Applicants were accorded fair hearing and 
adequate facilities to defend themselves. Each Applicant was presented with 
a charge sheet detailing the specific allegations against them and was given 
the opportunity to respond orally and in writing to the charges during the 
disciplinary sessions held in Dakar, Senegal, from 9th to 12th November 
2020. 
75. The Respondents deny all allegations of procedural irregularities or bias at the 
IDB. They assert that the conduct of disciplinary proceedings falls within the 
exclusive administrative discretion of the Respondents, and that it is not for 
the Applicants to determine the internal functioning, composition, or methods 
of the Disciplinary Board. 
ZOI Page 


76.At the conclusion of the proceedings, the Joint Disciplinary Board issued a 
detailed report containing its findings and recommendations for each 
implicated staff member. The report was duly adopted, and the 2nd 
Respondent, as Head of the Institution, gave reasons for the disciplinary 
sanctions in line with Article 71(d) of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations. 
• 
On the Legal Basis for Dismissal 
77. The Respondents contend that the Applicants' dismissals were justified under 
Articles 70( c) and 71 ( c) of the ECOW AS Staff Regulations, which provide 
that offences involving fraud, embezzlement, abuse of trust, or other acts 
detrimental to the Community attract third-degree sanctions, including 
summary dismissal with or without pension. 
78.The Respondents emphasize that the Applicants' actions constituted gross 
misconduct under the ECO WAS Revised Financial Regulations (2021 ), 
particularly Articles 9, 10, 13, and 39, which prescribe sanctions for financial 
malpractice, breach of fiduciary duty, and non-compliance with procurement 
procedures. 
79. They further rely on the Applicants' letters of appointment, which expressly 
empower the Respondent institution to dispense with notice in cases of serious 
misconduct warranting summary dismissal. 
80. The Respondents maintain that, following the IDB proceedings, the Council 
of Ministers reviewed and confirmed the dismissals by letters dated 8th 
February 2022, imposing the penalty of dismissal without pension rights and 
authorizing the initiation of legal proceedings to recover the misappropriated 
funds. 
• 
On Alleged Violation of Human Rights 
21 I Page 


81. The Respondents categorically deny that the Applicants' dismissals or 
disciplinary proceedings violated any of their fundamental human rights under 
the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights or any other international 
human rights instrument. They assert that the disciplinary process was fair, 
transparent, and consistent with the ECOW AS legal framework governing 
staff conduct. 
82. They submit that the Applicants' dismissals were administrative measures 
taken in good faith, following clear evidence of serious professional 
misconduct, and thus do not amount to a violation of the right to fair hearing 
or equal treatment as alleged. 
• 
On Procedural Objections 
83. The Respondents note that while the Applicants filed a motion for default 
judgment on 12th May 2023, the Respondents had already filed an application 
for extension of time and their joint Statement of Defence on 7th July 2023, 
supported by an affidavit of justification for delay. They therefore contend 
that the default judgment application is incompetent. 
84.They rely on the settled jurisprudence of this Honourable Court, notably 
MOHAMMED EL. TA YYIB BAH V. THE REPUBLIC OF SIERRA LEONE 
(2015) CCJELR, which holds that before granting a default judgment, the 
Court must satisfy itself that the application is admissible, that the procedural 
formalities have been observed, and that the application appears well-founded 
on its merits. 
• 
Conclusion of the Respondents ' Defence 
85 .In conclusion, the Respondents maintain that the dismissal of the three 
Applicants was lawful, procedurally compliant, and justified by the evidence 
221 Page 


presented. They submit that the Applicants' claims are frivolous, unfounde~ 
and lacking in merit, and accordingly urge this Honourable Court to dismiss 
the Application in its entirety. 
b. 
Pleas in law 
86.The Respondents are relying on the following pleas in support of their claims: 
1.

its officials." The crux of this provision is that the Court acts as a Public

ECOWAS Community institutions on the one hand, and their staff on the

101. 
Having established its jurisdiction, the Court proceeds to consider 
whether the application is admissible in accordance with the provisions

the merits, and serves to determine whether an applicant has the proper 
standing and has fulfilled the preliminary conditions required before seizing 
this, Court. 
102. 
In accordance with Article 10( e) of the Supplementary Protocol 
A/SP.1/01/05 (2005) amending the Protocol A/Pl/7/91, access to the Court is 
open to: "Staff of any Community Institution, after the staff member has 
exhausted all the appeal processes available to the officer under the ECO WAS 
Staff Rules and Regulations. "

The above provision imposes a mandatory condition precedent upon 
any staff member of a Community Institution seeking redress before this 
Court. Thus, unless and until a staff member has exhausted all the internal 
appeal mechanisms provided under the ECOWAS Staff Regulations, the 
Court cannot be properly seized of the matter. This position has been 
reaffirmed in several decisions of this Court, including PARLIAMENT OF 
ECOWAS v. COUNCIL OF MINISTERS OF ECOWAS (2005-2009) 
CCJELR at page 35, para.10, and NNAMDI F.C. CHUKWU v. ECOWAS

wishes to appeal against sanctions imposed on him/her must first write a letter 
to the Head of Institution requesting a review of his/her case ... Jfthe Head of

Institution maintains the decision, or if the staff member receives no response

within which to bring the matter to the notice of the Council of Ministers 
through the Staff Representative or the Head of Administration who shall 
present a memorandum in this connection to the Administration and Finance

evidence or recommendations from a competent committee. The Court therefore

infers that the decision was taken unilaterally and without proper record of 
justification. 
164. The Court recalls that the duty to communicate a reasoned decision is a 
fundamental procedural obligation, the breach of which deprives the staff 
member of both procedural fairness and substantive clarity. As held in 
MABOUDOU V. ECOWAS COMMISSION, (2016) CCJELR "the absence of 
a reasoned decision not only violates the Staff Regulations but also impedes the 
staff member's right to an effective remedy before this Court. " 
165. The Court further notes that the sanction of dismissal represents the most 
severe disciplinary measure under the ECOWAS Staff Regulations. Its 
imposition therefore requires strict adherence to both procedural safeguards and 
proportionality. Where the administration cannot show that lesser sanctions were 
considered or that dismissal was objectively justified, the Court will presume 
abuse of administrative power. 
166. In the present case, the Court recalls that no evidence has been adduced to 
demonstrate that the Respondent evaluated the alleged misconduct against 
available sanctions, or that the Applicants were afforded an opportunity to present 
mitigating factors before the decision was taken. The Court therefore concludes 
that the measure of dismissal was disproportionate, unreasoned, and 
communicated in violation of Articles 94 and 95 of the ECOWAS Staff 
Regulations. 
167. Accordingly, the Court finds that the dismissal of the Applicants was carried 
out in contravention of the applicable provisions of the ECOWAS Staff 
Regulations and the principles of fairness and proportionality inherent in 
administrative law. The absence of a written, reasoned decision and the failure to 
communicate the basis of the dismissal rendered the action arbitrary, 
procedurally defective, and legally unsustainable. 
431 Page 


b. 
Whether the Applicants are entitled to the reliefs sought 
168. Having found that the suspension and dismissal of the Applicants were not 
conducted in accordance with the ECOW AS Staff Regulations, the Court must 
now detennine whether the Applicants are entitled to the reliefs sought. 
169. The settled jurisprudence of this Court establishes that where a violation of a 
staff member's rights is established, the Court is empowered to grant appropriate 
remedies, including reinstatement, compensation for material and moral 
damages, and any other measure necessary to restore the victim to the position 
they would have been in had the violation not occurred. 
170. In TETEVI AMEGANVI V. ECOWAS PARLIAMENT (supra), the Court 
held that a declaration of unlawfulness, by itself, may not be sufficient redress 
where the wrongful act has caused pecuniary or moral injury. The Court thus 
emphasized its discretion to award just and equitable compensation to fully 
remedy the violation. The Court stated: "The power to award damages in cases 
of wrongful dismissal flows naturally from the Court's mandate to ensure the 
observance of law and justice within the CommunUy. " 
171. In assessing the entitlement to reliefs, the Court considers both material 
damages (loss of earnings, benefits, and entitlements directly resulting from the 
unlawful dismissal) and moral damages (emotional distress, humiliation, or 
damage to reputation occasioned by the Respondent's unlawful conduct). 
172. In the instant case, the Applicants have sought various reliefs, including: 
441 Page 
(a) A declaration that their suspension and dismissal were 
unlawful. 
(b) An order setting aside the said decisions. 
(c) Reinstatement to their former positions with full benefits. 


( d) Payment of all salaries and entitlements due from the date of 
suspension to the date of reinstatement or judgment; and 
( e) Compensation for moral damages occasioned by the unlawful 
dismissal. 
173. As to the declaratory relief, the Court having already found that the 
disciplinary process leading to the Applicants' dismissal was procedurally 
defective and contrary to Articles 92-95 of the ECOWAS Staff Regulations, the 
prayer for a declaration of unlawfulness is meritorious and is accordingly granted. 
174. As regards the prayer for reinstatement, the Court recalls its reasoning in 
KOULIBAL Y KASSOUM V. ECOWAS COMJ\.1ISSION (supra), where it held 
that reinstatement is the most appropriate remedy where the employment 
relationship has not been irretrievably damaged, and where institutional 
reorganization or loss of confidence does not render such reinstatement 
impracticable. The Court further held that where reinstatement is not feasible, 
compensation in lieu may be ordered. Considering the circumstances of the 
present case, including the nature of the Applicants' employment and the 
Respondent's failure to justify the disciplinary measures, the Court finds that 
reinstatement or, alternatively, full compensation in lieu thereof, constitutes the 
appropriate remedy. 
175. On the claim for payment of arrears of salaries and entitlements, the Court 
recalls its decision in DR. OUEDRAOGO V. ECO WAS COMMISSION (supra), 
wherein it held that a staff member who has been unlawfully suspended or 
dismissed is entitled to the restoration of all withheld emoluments from the date 
of suspension up to the date of reinstatement or final judgment. The rationale is 
that an unlawful dismissal produces no legal effect on the staff member's 
entitlements. 
45 I Page 


176. With respect to moral damages, the Court has consistently held that the 
arbitrary deprivation of employment, the attendant reputational harm, and 
emotional distress justify the award of compensation under Article 15 of the 1991 
Protocol (as amended), which empowers the Court to make appropriate orders 
for reparation. See MABOUDOU V. ECOW AS COMMISSION (2016) 
CCJELR and BRAHIM V. ECOWAS COMMISSION (supra). 
177. Taking all these factors into account, and applying the principle of 
proportionality, the Court finds that the Applicants have established their 
entitlement to the declaratory, restorative, and compensatory reliefs sought, as 
the Respondent's conduct resulted in both material and moral prejudice. 
178. Accordingly, the Court holds that: 
a. 
The Applicants' suspension and dismissal are unlawful, null, and 
void. 
b. The Respondent shall reinstate the Applicants to their respective 
positions or, where reinstatement is impracticable, pay the 
equivalent of twelve (12) months' gross salary in lieu of 
reinstatement. 
c. The Respondent shall pay the Applicants all arrears of salaries, 
allowances, and entitlements from the date of suspension to the date 
of this judgment; and 
d. The Respondent shall further pay each Applicant reasonable 
compensation for moral damages, to be assessed based on their 
individual circumstances and duration of wrongful deprivation. 
179. In conclusion, the Court finds that the Applicants are entitled to the reliefs 
sought, having established violations of the ECOWAS Staff. The Respondent's 
actions, being unlawful and arbitrary, warrant full redress to restore the 
Applicants to their lawful status and compensate for the injury suffered.

X. 
REPARATIONS 
180. The Court recalls that the three Applications have been duly consolidated by 
Order of this Court, and that the reliefs sought by the Applicants have accordingly 
been harmonised, with overlapping claims merged for coherence and 
consistency. 
181. In line with the Court's earlier findings that the suspension and subsequent 
dismissal of the Applicants were carried out in violation of the ECO WAS Staff 
Regulations the Court now proceeds to determine the appropriate measures of 
reparation arising therefrom. 
182. In view of the evidence before the Court and the findings on the merits, the 
Court declares that the dismissal of the Applicants by the Respondents was 
arbitrary, unlawful, null and void, having been executed in violation of the 
ECOWAS Staff Regulations; and the suspension and/or stoppage of the 
Applicants' salaries, allowances and entitlements prior to the exhaustion of the 
internal appeal process was unlawful and contrary to Article 68(b) of the 
ECOWAS Staff Regulations. 
183. However, the Court turns to its well-reasoned ratio in DR. ROSE 
MBATOMON AKO V WEST AFRICAN MONETARY AGENCY AND 5 
ORS. (2013) CCJELR, that a master has a right to determine the employment of 
his servant for good or bad cause, or for no cause at all. Where there is such a 
wrongful dismissal or termination of service, legal authorities are numerous that 
the only remedy is the claim for damages for that wrongful dismissal or 
termination. This has always been based on the notion that no servant, however 
willing, can be imposed by the court, on an unwilling master even where the 
master's behavior is wrong except in exceptional circumstances. The employer 
is only liable for his wrongful acts in damages and nothing more.

185. The Court, therefore, after considering the facts and evidence before it finds 
that each Applicant should be awarded compensation for material and moral
